comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes augmenting probabilistic deep generative models (DGMs) with external hierarchical memory and an associated soft attention mechanism to recover local, variant details lost during bottom-up abstraction. The generative network (P-Net) uses memory at deterministic layers to produce richer reconstructions while the recognition network (Q-Net) remains memory-free and asymmetric to reduce competition between inference and generation. The memory is parameterized as slot matrices accessed by sigmoid-based attention; combination functions fuse memory and top-down signals. Training uses SGVB/IWAE variational objectives with optional local reconstruction penalties. Experiments on MNIST, OCR-letters and Frey faces show improved log-likelihoods, qualitatively better samples, and improved missing-value imputation versus VAE/IWAE baselines.

Strengths:
- The idea of using an external memory as a top-down auxiliary to recover local detail in DGMs is well motivated and, to my reading of the text, novel in the context of probabilistic generative modeling.
- The design keeps the recognition model compact (memory-free) which is sensible for disentangling invariant inference from detailed generation.
- The proposed components (memory, attention, combine function) are differentiable and can be trained end-to-end within the variational framework — a practical advantage.
- Empirical results demonstrate consistent gains in held-out log-likelihood and sample quality on several standard small-image datasets.

Weaknesses and concerns / Suggestions for improvement:
1. Scope of experiments: All evaluations are on small, low-resolution image datasets (MNIST, OCR-letters, Frey faces). It is unclear how well the memory mechanism scales to higher-resolution, natural images or more complex datasets. Adding experiments (or at least discussion and complexity analysis) for larger images, or showing how to integrate memory with convolutional architectures would strengthen the claim of general utility.
2. Attention parametrization: The memory addressing uses sigmoid activations producing unnormalized slot coefficients. This is an unusual choice compared to softmax attention or other normalized attentions. The paper should justify this choice empirically or include comparisons (sigmoid vs softmax vs hard attention) and discuss when normalization is preferable.
3. Memory update / write mechanism: The memory is trained only via optimization of parameters (i.e., static matrices updated by gradients), not via a dynamic write/read procedure per example. This limits expressiveness; it would help to discuss trade-offs and whether add-on dynamic write rules were considered.
4. Ablation and sensitivity studies: The paper includes some analysis (e.g., disabling memory) but lacks systematic ablations: effects of total memory slots, per-layer slot counts, choice of combination function, and role of the local reconstruction regularizer (only a couple of brief mentions). More controlled ablations and learning curves would be informative.
5. Comparisons with broader baselines: The paper compares primarily to VAE/IWAE and cites autoregressive models and GAN variants but does not include direct comparisons (quantitative or qualitative) to autoregressive density models or modern adversarial generators on similar tasks. The claim of state-of-the-art should be qualified (the paper already notes permutation-invariant architectures) and contexts of benchmarks clarified.
6. Computational cost and parameters: Although the authors state they control parameter counts via slot limits, concrete numbers for training time, memory overhead, and runtimes compared to baselines are missing. These are important for assessing practical adoption.
7. Presentation issues: The manuscript contains duplicated and garbled paragraphs near the end and some typos; polishing is needed for readability. Some design choices (initialization heuristics, why ReLU, why particular λ values) are not fully justified and could be explained briefly.

Overall assessment:
The paper presents an interesting and seemingly effective extension to DGMs by introducing external memory and attention in the generative path, coupled with an asymmetric recognition network. The idea is original and experimentally promising on small-scale image tasks. However, the evaluated scope is limited, and several important questions remain (attention normalization, scalability, ablations, computational overhead). Addressing these issues would increase confidence in the broader impact of the method.

Concrete recommendations for revision:
- Add ablation experiments on memory size, attention choices (sigmoid vs softmax), and the combination function.
- Provide runtime and parameter count comparisons and discuss scalability to higher-resolution data and convolutional architectures.
- Clean up duplicated text and clarify all design/initialization choices.
- If possible, include at least one experiment on a more challenging image dataset or on a convolutional variant to demonstrate broader applicability.

Given the novelty, solid experimental gains on standard benchmarks, and the reasonable methodological soundness, I recommend acceptance (high score).
